title: Pain d'épi
subtitle: A baguette-weight loaf scored into a wheat-ear shape before baking
excerpt: Pain d'épi is a baguette dough that the baker cuts into a zigzag wheat-ear shape with scissors just before it goes into the oven. Each snip is pulled to one side so the loaf fans out into separate rolls joined at the base. The scoring aerates the dough and gives every piece a generous crust ratio. The shaping takes about two minutes once the dough is proven.
sourceNotes: The épi shape derives from classic French boulangerie tradition; the word épi means ear or spike of wheat. The dough is a standard baguette formula (strong or French-type flour, 65-68% hydration, 0.3-0.5% yeast). Technique described in Raymond Calvel, 'The Taste of Bread' (1990 French edition; English translation 2001). Pre-1930s bread-baking guides in Paris archives describe shaped wheat loaves for harvest celebrations.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the baked épi as individual rolls. Refresh from frozen at 200 degrees C fan for 6 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The shaped épi can prove in the fridge overnight, covered. Remove 30 minutes before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven with a water tray; drop to 210 degrees C after 5 minutes
baking.steamMethod: water tray on the oven floor for the first 5 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: A 20–30 minute rest after mixing flour and water before adding salt and yeast. During the rest, the flour hydrates fully and gluten begins to develop without mechanical work. The result is a more extensible, smoother dough that requires less kneading and shapes more easily.

content.text: The scissors are the recipe. Make the baguette dough as you normally would, then at the moment of shaping, take scissors and cut diagonally along the length of the baton, pulling each piece to alternate sides. The épi bakes as a row of linked rolls, each with a pointed tip and a high crust-to-crumb ratio. It is better as tear-and-share bread than a baguette would be at a dinner table.

rest of 20 minutes before adding the yeast and salt makes the dough more extensible and easier to shape into the long baton. Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more; use an oven thermometer and preheat the tray.

Mix 350 g strong bread flour with 240 ml tepid water until no dry flour remains. Rest for 20 minutes: this is the autolyse. Add 1.4 g yeast and 7 g salt and work them in by squeezing the dough between your fingers for 3 to 4 minutes, then knead on a clean surface for a further 5 minutes until smooth and springy.
Oil the bowl lightly and return the dough. Cover and leave at room temperature until roughly doubled, about 60 minutes. The dough should feel airy when you press a finger in.

Turn the dough onto a very lightly floured surface. Flatten gently into a rough rectangle, fold the long edge towards the centre, then roll it under your hands into a baton about 40 cm long. Transfer to a parchment-lined baking tray, seam-side down. Cover loosely and prove for 25 to 35 minutes until puffy but not doubled.

Heat the oven to 230 degrees C conventional with a water tray on the bottom shelf. Hold the scissors at a 45-degree angle to the baton and make a cut almost but not quite through to the base, about 4 cm from the top end. Pull that segment to the left. Move 4 cm down and make the next cut, pulling to the right. Continue alternating left and right until you reach the end, leaving the tip pointing up. Each segment should be a separate roll, still attached at the base.

Slide into the oven and pour a small cup of water into the water tray to create steam. After 5 minutes, reduce to 210 degrees C and remove the water tray. Bake for a further 15 to 17 minutes until the crust is deep golden, the tips are lightly charred, and each roll sounds hollow when tapped. Cool on a rack for 10 minutes; the crust is best within an hour of baking.

items.problem: Rolls fused back together in the oven
items.solution: Each cut was not pulled far enough to the side, or the scissors were at too shallow an angle. Aim to pull each segment at least 3 cm from the centreline. The dough expands sideways during baking; it will close slightly but the separation should hold.

items.problem: Épi spread flat instead of rising
items.solution: The dough was over-proofed before cutting. Prove only until puffy, not doubled. An over-proofed baguette dough is weak and cannot hold the scissor-cut shape.

items.problem: Pale crust, soft rather than crisp
items.solution: The steam was absent or the oven temperature was too low. The initial steam is what gives the crust its thin, crisp character. Domestic ovens drift; use an oven thermometer.
